agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer notifications.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature touches at minimum four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, and stop time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, zone assignments, and shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed), zone assignment of parcels, and parcel attributes like size/weight class; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, and customer communication preferences. Each context must communicate via well-defined domain events, not direct coupling.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or complete all assigned deliveries. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time or distance for a driver within their assigned zone during their active shift." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area used to partition deliveries and assign drivers. A delivery belongs to exactly one zone. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route. It exists within a specific zone." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (recipient unavailable, address inaccessible, etc.), triggering removal from the current route and potential reassignment or return-to-depot logic." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The process of recomputing an optimized route in response to a material change: new delivery added, delivery failure, or driver deviation from suggested route." },
      { "term": "Driver Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence materially diverges from the suggested route, indicating they are no longer following the optimized plan." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity notification threshold: the set of the next three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. When a delivery enters this window, the associated customer is notified." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of a driver's vehicle (e.g., motorcycle, van, small truck, freight truck) that constrains which deliveries they may be assigned. Derived from the physical vehicle and the driver's licensing." },
      { "term": "Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement", "definition": "A property of a delivery/parcel that specifies the minimum or required vehicle class for transport (e.g., freight requires a freight-class vehicle)." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration Estimate", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal stakeholders with authority to configure operational parameters such as per-zone stop duration overrides." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system MUST automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational workflow trigger — the business could decide to allow manual route creation or batch assignment instead. It is a policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver starts a shift in Zone A with 10 pending deliveries, an optimized route containing all 10 deliveries is generated and assigned to the driver within the shift-start transaction."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose vehicle class requirement exceeds the driver's licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight. Assigning such a delivery would be unsafe, illegal, and undeliverable. If this invariant is violated, the system is in a corrupt state — the delivery is guaranteed to fail and may expose the company to liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any attempt to assign a delivery with vehicle class requirement 'freight' to a driver with licensed vehicle class 'motorcycle' MUST be rejected by the domain model, regardless of the calling code path (route optimizer, manual override, API)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone during their shift.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy for route freshness. The business could decide to batch recalculations or defer them. The system is not corrupt if recalculation is delayed — just suboptimal.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver has an active route in Zone A, when a new delivery is created in Zone A, a RouteRecalculationRequested event is emitted and the route is recomputed to include the new delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational policy — the system remains consistent without recalculation; the driver simply has a suboptimal route with a gap. Not a data integrity issue.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver has an active route with deliveries [D1, D2, D3, D4], when D2 is marked as failed, the route is recalculated to optimize the remaining stops [D1, D3, D4]."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational policy — deviation detection thresholds and recalculation frequency are tunable business parameters.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver's active route suggests stop sequence [D1, D2, D3] and the driver skips D1 and proceeds to D2, the system detects the deviation and recalculates the route from the driver's current position."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a notification policy. The window size (3) is a business decision, and notification preferences may vary. No data corruption if notification is delayed or window size changes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver completes stop N, and delivery D is now at position N+1, N+2, or N+3 in the route, and D's customer has not yet been notified for this delivery, then a CustomerDeliveryApproaching notification is sent to D's customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated time per delivery stop is 4 minutes. This default can be overridden per zone by the operations team.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a configurable operational parameter, explicitly designed to be changeable by the operations team.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given no zone-level override exists, the route optimizer uses 4 minutes per stop. Given Zone A has an override of 6 minutes, the route optimizer uses 6 minutes for all stops in Zone A."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A driver can only be assigned deliveries within their assigned zone for the current shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone boundaries define operational territories, dispatching agreements, and potentially regulatory/insurance boundaries. Cross-zone assignment breaks the fundamental partitioning model and could result in overlapping routes, SLA violations, and accountability gaps. If a driver has deliveries from two zones, the zone model is corrupted.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any attempt to add a delivery from Zone B to a driver assigned to Zone A MUST be rejected, regardless of the code path."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A route recalculation MUST NOT reorder already-completed stops or re-include failed deliveries.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Completed and failed deliveries have terminal states. Re-including them would corrupt the delivery lifecycle — a completed delivery cannot be delivered again, and a failed delivery requires a separate business process (retry scheduling, return-to-sender) before re-entering a route.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a route with [D1(completed), D2(failed), D3(pending), D4(pending)], after recalculation the new route contains only [D3, D4] (possibly reordered) and never D1 or D2."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and is assigned to Zone A, which has 5 pending deliveries all compatible with the driver's vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route containing all 5 deliveries is generated and assigned to the driver, with stop order minimizing total travel time/distance"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver with vehicle class 'motorcycle' starts a shift in a zone containing 3 motorcycle-compatible deliveries and 1 freight delivery",
      "when": "The route is generated",
      "then": "The route contains only the 3 compatible deliveries; the freight delivery is excluded and remains in the pending pool for a qualified driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route [D1, D2, D3] and a new delivery D4 is added to their zone, compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "D4 is confirmed as pending in the driver's zone",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include D4 in the optimal position among the remaining unvisited stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route [D1, D2, D3] and D2 is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated with only [D1, D3] (plus any other pending stops), and D2 is removed permanently from this route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver is on route [D1, D2, D3, D4, D5] and completes D1",
      "when": "D1 is marked completed",
      "then": "Customers for D2, D3, and D4 (the next 3 stops) receive a 'delivery approaching' notification, if not already notified"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver deviates from the suggested route by visiting D3 before D2",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current state, reoptimizing the remaining unvisited stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone A has a stop duration override of 6 minutes configured by the operations team",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A",
      "then": "Each stop in the route uses 6 minutes as the estimated stop duration, not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and a new delivery is added to a different zone",
      "when": "The delivery is created",
      "then": "The driver's route is NOT recalculated and the delivery is NOT added to their route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A customer has already been notified that their delivery is in the 'next 3 stops' window",
      "when": "A route recalculation occurs and their delivery is still within the next 3 stops",
      "then": "The customer is NOT notified again (idempotent notification)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A route recalculation moves a customer's delivery OUT of the 'next 3 stops' window after they were already notified",
      "when": "The recalculation completes",
      "then": "The customer receives an updated notification informing them of the revised estimate (the original notification created an expectation that must be managed)"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift but there are zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "Driver is assigned an empty route. The system should listen for new deliveries in the zone and trigger route creation (not just recalculation) when the first delivery appears." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver possesses", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries remain in the pending pool unassigned. An alert should be raised to the operations team indicating an unserviceable demand-supply mismatch." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple drivers start shifts simultaneously in the same zone", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries must be partitioned across drivers — not duplicated. The optimization must account for multiple active drivers in a zone, treating it as a vehicle routing problem (VRP), not independent single-driver TSP instances." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid successive recalculation triggers (e.g., 5 new deliveries added in 10 seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Recalculations should be debounced or batched to avoid thrashing. The system should not recalculate 5 times — it should coalesce triggers and recalculate once with all changes." },
    { "scenario": "Driver completes all deliveries mid-shift and new deliveries arrive later", "expected_behavior": "The driver is in an idle state with an empty route. New deliveries in their zone should trigger a fresh route assignment, not a recalculation of a completed route." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another while a driver is mid-route", "expected_behavior": "If the delivery was on the driver's active route, it must be removed and the route recalculated. The receiving zone's active drivers should have their routes recalculated to include it." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification sent for 'next 3 stops' but driver's shift ends before reaching that stop", "expected_behavior": "The customer must receive a corrective notification that their delivery has been deferred. The delivery returns to pending status for the next available driver." },
    { "scenario": "Route recalculation produces a route where the driver must leave and re-enter their zone due to road geometry", "expected_behavior": "The route optimizer should optimize for actual road network travel, not straight-line zone containment. Brief geographic exit from zone boundaries during transit between stops is acceptable." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team changes the stop duration override for a zone while drivers are mid-route", "expected_behavior": "Active routes should use the updated stop duration on next recalculation. The system should not retroactively adjust completed stop timestamps, but future ETA calculations should reflect the new value." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery marked as failed is immediately retried by the operations team", "expected_behavior": "The retry creates a new delivery attempt with its own lifecycle. It re-enters the pending pool and may be assigned to the same or different driver on next route calculation. The failed record remains immutable." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly impacts fuel costs, deliveries-per-shift throughput, and driver labor costs. A well-optimized route can improve deliveries per shift by 15-30%. Incorrect vehicle class assignment could result in failed deliveries costing $15-50 each in re-delivery attempts. Customer notification accuracy affects customer retention and reduces 'where is my package' support calls (typically $3-7 per call).",
    "operational": "Dynamic recalculation introduces computational load proportional to delivery volume × recalculation frequency. Debouncing is critical to avoid cascading recomputations. The operations team gains direct control over stop duration estimates, enabling zone-specific tuning without engineering changes. Multi-driver zone scenarios require careful delivery partitioning to avoid double-assignment.",
    "user": "Drivers receive clear, optimized routes reducing cognitive load and windshield time. Customers gain visibility into delivery proximity, reducing anxiety and missed deliveries. However, frequent route recalculations may frustrate drivers if the next stop keeps changing. The 'next 3 stops' notification sets a concrete customer expectation that MUST be honored or actively corrected.",
    "risk": "Primary risks: (1) Vehicle class constraint violation is a safety/legal risk — must be treated as an invariant. (2) Notification-then-recalculation creates a promise-breaking pattern if not handled carefully. (3) Concurrent recalculation triggers may cause race conditions leading to stale or conflicting route states. (4) Multi-driver zones without proper delivery partitioning risk double-delivery or undelivered parcels. (5) Deviation detection false positives (GPS jitter, tunnels) triggering unnecessary recalculations.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Gain automated route guidance reducing decision fatigue. Risk frustration if recalculations are too frequent or if the system incorrectly detects deviations." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Gain proactive delivery proximity notifications. Risk broken expectations if notifications are sent but delivery timing changes significantly after notification." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gain per-zone stop duration control. Need tooling to monitor route quality, handle unserviceable delivery backlogs, and manage zone configuration." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Reduced manual route planning workload but may lose granular control. Need override capabilities for edge cases the optimizer cannot handle." },
      { "group": "Finance/Management", "impact": "Expected improvement in cost-per-delivery and deliveries-per-shift metrics. Need dashboards showing optimization impact vs. baseline." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 62,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well and is internally consistent — no contradictions detected. However, several critical domain questions are unaddressed: multi-driver zone behavior (VRP vs. TSP), deviation detection thresholds, notification correction policy when routes change after notification, and the exact semantics of vehicle class hierarchy. The spec also does not define what 'optimized' means (minimize time? distance? priority-weighted?). These gaps don't block initial implementation but will cause ambiguity in production edge cases.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — clear physical and legal basis",
      "Three recalculation triggers are well-defined and unambiguous",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is straightforward",
      "Customer notification threshold (next 3 stops) is concrete"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver zone delivery partitioning strategy is unspecified",
      "Deviation detection criteria (GPS threshold, sequence deviation, time deviation) are undefined",
      "Notification correction policy when recalculation invalidates a prior notification",
      "Optimization objective function (time vs. distance vs. priority) is unspecified",
      "Vehicle class hierarchy and compatibility rules (can a van driver take motorcycle-class deliveries?) are not defined"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift (spec implies but does not state explicitly)",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is strict equality or hierarchical (larger vehicles can handle smaller deliveries) — spec is ambiguous",
      "Route optimization means minimizing total travel time, the most common objective in last-mile delivery",
      "The 'next 3 stops' notification is sent once per delivery per route, not re-sent on every recalculation",
      "Deviation detection is based on the driver visiting stops out of sequence, not purely GPS drift"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define behavior when multiple drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously. Route optimization for a single driver is a Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP); for multiple drivers it becomes a Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) with fundamentally different algorithms and delivery partitioning logic. This is not a minor detail — it changes the entire optimization architecture.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: Each driver gets an independent TSP over the full pending set (risk: duplicate assignments)", "B: Deliveries are partitioned across active drivers then each gets a TSP (need partitioning strategy)", "C: Full VRP solver assigns and routes all drivers simultaneously"],
      "recommendation": "Option C (VRP) is the correct domain model, but Option B is a pragmatic first step. Require product team to clarify before implementation."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Vehicle class compatibility direction is unspecified. Can a freight truck driver deliver a parcel that only requires a motorcycle? The spec says 'a motorcycle driver cannot be assigned a freight shipment' but does not state whether larger vehicles can handle smaller delivery classes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": ["A: Strict match — driver vehicle class must exactly match delivery requirement", "B: Hierarchical — larger vehicle classes can fulfill smaller class requirements (motorcycle < van < truck < freight)"],
      "recommendation": "Option B (hierarchical) aligns with physical reality — a van can carry anything a motorcycle can. But product team must confirm, as there may be business reasons for strict matching (e.g., cost efficiency, motorcycle-only zones)."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What constitutes 'driver deviation' is undefined. Without clear thresholds, this trigger could fire constantly (GPS noise) or never (threshold too high). This is a critical operational parameter.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: Sequence-based — deviation means visiting stops out of the suggested order", "B: GPS-based — deviation means the driver is more than X meters from the expected route path", "C: Both — any sequence skip OR significant geographic departure triggers recalculation"],
      "recommendation": "Option A (sequence-based) is the most reliable and deterministic. GPS-based detection is noisy and requires tuning. Recommend starting with sequence deviation only."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The optimization objective function is not specified. 'Optimized route' could mean minimize total distance, minimize total time (accounting for traffic), maximize delivery priority compliance, or a weighted combination. This fundamentally changes the routing algorithm output.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team", "Finance/Management"],
      "options": ["Define optimization as minimize total travel time", "Define as minimize distance", "Define as weighted multi-objective (time + priority + SLA deadlines)"],
      "recommendation": "Minimize total travel time is the industry standard for last-mile delivery. But if deliveries have SLA deadlines or priority tiers, those constraints must be incorporated. Need product team to confirm."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
